content.text: I'll start by reading the problem carefully and extracting the example.

content.content:
# Day 9: Movie Theater

You slide down the firepole in the corner of the playground and land in the North Pole base movie theater!

The movie theater has a big tile floor with an interesting pattern. Elves here are redecorating the theater by switching out some of the square tiles in the big grid they form. Some of the tiles are red; the Elves would like to find the largest rectangle that uses red tiles for two of its opposite corners. They even have a list of where the red tiles are located in the grid (your puzzle input).

For example:

7,1
11,1
11,7
9,7
9,5
2,5
2,3
7,3

Showing red tiles as # and other tiles as ., the above arrangement of red tiles would look like this:

..............
.......#...#..
..............
..#....#......
..............
..#......#....
..............
.........#.#..
..............

You can choose any two red tiles as the opposite corners of your rectangle; your goal is to find the largest rectangle possible.

For example, you could make a rectangle (shown as O) with an area of 24 between 2,5 and 9,7:

..............
.......#...#..
..............
..#....#......
..............
..OOOOOOOO....
..OOOOOOOO....
..OOOOOOOO.#..
..............

Or, you could make a rectangle with area 35 between 7,1 and 11,7:

..............
.......OOOOO..
.......OOOOO..
..#....OOOOO..
.......OOOOO..
..#....OOOOO..
.......OOOOO..
.......OOOOO..
..............

You could even make a thin rectangle with an area of only 6 between 7,3 and 2,3:

..............
.......#...#..
..............
..OOOOOO......
..............
..#......#....
..............
.........#.#..
..............

Ultimately, the largest rectangle you can make in this example has area 50. One way to do this is between 2,5 and 11,1:

..............
..OOOOOOOOOO..
..OOOOOOOOOO..
..OOOOOOOOOO..
..OOOOOOOOOO..
..OOOOOOOOOO..
..............
.........#.#..
..............

Using two red tiles as opposite corners, what is the largest area of any rectangle you can make?

--- Part Two ---

The Elves just remembered: they can only switch out tiles that are red or green. So, your rectangle can only include red or green tiles.

In your list, every red tile is connected to the red tile before and after it by a straight line of green tiles. The list wraps, so the first red tile is also connected to the last red tile. Tiles that are adjacent in your list will always be on either the same row or the same column.

Using the same example as before, the tiles marked X would be green:

..............
.......#XXX#..
.......X...X..
..#XXXX#...X..
..X........X..
..#XXXXXX#.X..
.........X.X..
.........#X#..
..............

In addition, all of the tiles inside this loop of red and green tiles are also green. So, in this example, these are the green tiles:

..............
.......#XXX#..
.......XXXXX..
..#XXXX#XXXX..
..XXXXXXXXXX..
..#XXXXXX#XX..
.........XXX..
.........#X#..
..............

The remaining tiles are never red nor green.

The rectangle you choose still must have red tiles in opposite corners, but any other tiles it includes must now be red or green. This significantly limits your options.

For example, you could make a rectangle out of red and green tiles with an area of 15 between 7,3 and 11,1:

..............
.......OOOOO..
.......OOOOO..
..#XXXXOOOOO..
..XXXXXXXXXX..
..#XXXXXX#XX..
.........XXX..
.........#X#..
..............

Or, you could make a thin rectangle with an area of 3 between 9,7 and 9,5:

..............
.......#XXX#..
.......XXXXX..
..#XXXX#XXXX..
..XXXXXXXXXX..
..#XXXXXXOXX..
.........OXX..
.........OX#..
..............

The largest rectangle you can make in this example using only red and green tiles has area 24. One way to do this is between 9,5 and 2,3:

..............
.......#XXX#..
.......XXXXX..
..OOOOOOOOXX..
..OOOOOOOOXX..
..OOOOOOOOXX..
.........XXX..
.........#X#..
..............

Using two red tiles as opposite corners, what is the largest area of any rectangle you can make using only red and green tiles?

content.text:
Now let me plan my approach:

**Part 1:** Find the largest rectangle whose opposite corners are both red tiles. Area = |x2-x1+1| * |y2-y1+1| (inclusive). Just check all pairs of red tiles.

**Part 2:** The red tiles form a polygon (connected in order, wrapping). Green tiles are on the edges and interior. A rectangle with red opposite corners is valid only if all tiles in it are red or green (inside/on the polygon). I need to determine which tiles are inside the polygon using ray casting or a grid-based approach, then find the largest rectangle with red corners that's entirely within the red/green region.

Let me think more carefully:
- The polygon is axis-aligned (rectilinear)
- "Inside" tiles include polygon interior + boundary
- For Part 2: rectangle [x1,x2]×[y1,y2] must have all cells be red or green, and corners (x1,y1), (x2,y2) must be red

For large inputs, I need efficient approaches. Let me check input size first.

input.command: wc -l input.txt && cat input.txt | awk -F',' '{print $1, $2}' | sort -n | tail -5 && cat input.txt | awk -F',' '{print $1, $2}' | sort -k2 -n | tail -5
cat input.txt | awk -F',' '{print $1}' | sort -n | head -3 && cat input.txt | awk -F',' '{print $2}' | sort -n | head -3

content.text:
496 red tiles, coordinates up to ~98k. Grid is too large to store directly. I need coordinate compression.

**Plan:**

**Part 1:** With 496 red tiles, O(N²) pairs = ~123k pairs. For each pair, area = (|dx|+1)*(|dy|+1). Find max. Simple.

**Part 2:** Need to check if entire rectangle is red/green. The polygon is rectilinear. Use coordinate compression to work with compressed grid. For each cell in compressed grid, determine if it's inside/on the polygon (use scanline/ray casting). Then for each pair of red corners, check if their rectangle is entirely red/green in the compressed grid.

With 496 points and coordinate compression, the compressed grid is at most 496×496. The polygon interior check via ray casting at compressed coordinates. Then checking all pairs of red corners: O(N²) pairs, each check needs to verify all compressed cells in the rectangle are valid - this could be O(N²) per check = O(N⁴) total which is too slow.

Better: Use 2D prefix sums on the compressed grid to check if a rectangle is all-valid in O(1).

Now let me think about the coordinate compression approach more carefully.

The key insight for Part 2: The polygon is rectilinear. I'll use coordinate compression. For each compressed cell, I determine if it's "valid" (red or green = inside or on boundary of polygon). Then I use 2D prefix sums to check rectangles.

But wait - I need to check if a rectangle in REAL coordinates has all cells valid. A compressed cell (ci, cj) represents a rectangle of real cells [xs[ci], xs[ci+1]-1] × [ys[cj], ys[cj+1]-1]. If a compressed cell is valid, ALL real cells in it are valid. So I can check validity in compressed space.

For a pair of red tiles (x1,y1) and (x2,y2), the real rectangle spans from min to max. I need every compressed cell in that range to be valid.

With prefix sums of "invalid compressed cells", I can check in O(1) if any compressed cell in a range is invalid. Then for each pair of red tiles, check O(1). Total: O(N²). 

But actually checking "invalid cells" sum = 0 isn't quite right since compressed cells represent different sizes. What I actually need: sum of invalid REAL cells in rectangle = 0.

Let me think differently: for each compressed cell, mark it 0 if all-valid, or 1 if any invalid. Then prefix sum on this. If sum in the compressed rectangle is 0, then all cells are valid.

Actually I need to be more careful. A compressed cell might be partially valid... but for a rectilinear polygon, entire compressed cells should be uniformly inside or outside. Let me verify this assumption: coordinate compression divides the plane at the x and y coordinates of vertices. Between vertices, the inside/outside status doesn't change. So yes, each compressed cell is uniformly valid or invalid.

input.command:
# Test with example
cp input.txt input_real.txt
cp example.txt input.txt
./main
cp input_real.txt input.txt

content.text:
Part 1 is correct (50). Part 2 should be 24, but I got 21. Let me debug.

The issue is with how I'm checking if the rectangle is fully inside the polygon. Let me visualize the valid cells for the example.

There are two issues:
1. My ray casting is wrong - the interior detection is off. The real grid doesn't match the expected.
2. (7,4) is incorrectly marked INVALID when it should be valid (it's interior).

The problem is with my ray casting approach for rectilinear polygons. Let me reconsider. Looking at the expected output vs my output - my boundary detection is wrong. The boundary should include the edges between red tiles (including horizontal edges), but I'm marking the horizontal edges as boundary which is wrong when using "ray upward" casting.

The standard issue: when the ray passes through a vertex or along a horizontal edge, we need special handling. Let me use a different convention: cast ray to the left and only count vertical edges.

content.text: The ray casting approach works now. I need to fix the main code with this corrected approach: cast ray to the LEFT, count only vertical edge crossings using `ymin <= py < ymax` convention.

content.text:
Both parts work correctly on the example and run quickly on the real input (0.3 seconds). Let me verify the logic once more:

- **Part 1**: O(N²) pairs of red tiles, find max area rectangle with them as opposite corners. ✓
- **Part 2**: Coordinate compression of the rectilinear polygon formed by red tiles (connected in order). Ray casting to determine inside/outside. 2D prefix sums on compressed validity grid to check O(1) if any rectangle is entirely valid. ✓

Part 2: CORRECT
